keyEvents.impact: Africa's most populous state born with deep regional, ethnic, and religious fault lines
Secessionist war killed over a million; national unity became the supreme political imperative
Petroleum wealth fueled growth but entrenched corruption, rent-seeking, and neglect of agriculture
Cycles of military rule normalized the army's political role and weakened civilian institutions

keyEvents.event: Return to democracy under the Fourth Republic
keyEvents.impact: Civilian rule restored with an informal north-south power rotation to manage regional rivalry
Jihadist violence devastated the northeast; banditry and separatism spread insecurity nationwide
Youth-led uprising against police brutality exposed a deep generational legitimacy gap

history.narrative: The giant of Africa aspires to continental leadership through ECOWAS and sheer demographic weight, but is consumed by internal insecurity, oil dependence, and the perpetual balancing act between its Muslim north and Christian south. It plays the US, China, and Russia against each other for investment and arms while guarding its autonomy.

redLines.description: Secessionist breakup of the federation (Biafra redux)
Foreign seizure or blockade of Niger Delta oil infrastructure
Foreign sponsorship of jihadist or separatist insurgents on Nigerian soil

internalDivisions.name: Hausa-Fulani, Yoruba, and Igbo rivalry with Igbo separatist undercurrent
Northeast insurgency, northwest banditry, and Delta militancy

goals.description: Defeat Boko Haram, ISWAP, and banditry to restore control of the north
Diversify beyond oil, fix power and refining capacity, and create jobs for a booming population
Lead West Africa through ECOWAS and claim Africa's seat at global tables
Hold the federation together against separatist and communal pressures
